Soya

messages.body:
Hi Professor,

This is Robin from the Humor project, MIchael told me Akify is trying to
raise. My friend and fellow Columbia MSCS student Christian
<https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://www.linkedin.com/in/christianmmontgomery/__;!!BDUfV1Et5lrpZQ!Tn0syRwEeRTbGpSkflTH-ANu8lTSNKFT52RZJCg860AjUGSO44P-x-pB65xtdIi4T0DCS0nUErH8I76VckGc73yz$ > I feel would be a huge
asset for you guys to talk to.

He has deep experience across investing, strategic finance, and
storytelling for tech companies from investment banking and venture
investing to leading GTM strategy at Weights & Biases (the AI dev platform)

He’s great at shaping narratives that resonate with investors and I feel
like *he would be so valuable in terms of shaping a story that would be
digestible for investors.*

Please let me know if you would like to meet and I'll set it up right away!

Robin

threads.subject: Re: Soya(Akify) <> Choongsoon(Top Harvest)

messages.subject: Soya(Akify) <> Choongsoon(Top Harvest)
messages.body:
Hi Soya and Lydia,

It was great meeting you and thank you so much for taking the time to meet
with me.
After our internal review, we've decided that this round would be out of
scope for us.
We were impressed with several aspects of Akify, particularly: 1) The
"minimal friction" approach to human-in-the-loop enterprise automation, 2)
the high quality of your initial customer traction.

Ultimately, our hesitation is focused on seeing more evidence of
product-market fit beyond these early adopters and understanding the
long-term defensibility. As you know, this is the core challenge and
opportunity of being at such an early stage.
We prefer to see more traction on these fronts, even if it means waiting
for the next round (with a higher valuation and of course risk not being
able to get in). It's a risk-reward calculus.
I'll be cheering you on from the sidelines and would welcome the chance to
stay in touch to follow your progress.

I wanted to get back to you as candidly and as quickly as possible.
Thanks again for your time and I wish you the best with fundraising.

Best,

-------------------------------------------------
*Choongsoon Bae*
*General Partner, Top Harvest Capital <https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://www.topharvestcap.com/__;!!BDUfV1Et5lrpZQ!U2d5CBXrdi4CLshCgngDnjX3vRuRG9zvQqi_HGkNA8zq_UPw6Ie8KHbZLZkIZus3VnORKymPtX7CiWg6ojqD$ >*
*LinkedIn <https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://www.linkedin.com/in/choongsoon-bae-32b3134/__;!!BDUfV1Et5lrpZQ!U2d5CBXrdi4CLshCgngDnjX3vRuRG9zvQqi_HGkNA8zq_UPw6Ie8KHbZLZkIZus3VnORKymPtX7Ciev15MqH$ >*

threads.subject: Akify

messages.subject: Akify
messages.body:
Lets be careful about how to talk about akify.   Akify can be viewed as a
competitor to some partners.   We should be clear about the line between
the research and company.   For instance, zoom would not partner with us
since it may see itself in competition with the entire lab.

threads.subject: Re: Akify materials

messages.subject: Akify materials
messages.body:
Hi Soya and Lydia,

I really appreciated getting to meet and learn more about your work. I reviewed with Chris and he agreed with the concern that Akify may be too close to some of our current portfolio companies. As a result, it would not be appropriate to directly participate at this time.

However, we are excited by what you are doing and would like to be helpful if we can be. The two things related to this that usually are most relevant are (a) if there are ways to help connect you with investors who are aligned with your interests/focus and can be of help to you; (b) if there are people (potential partners/collaborators/customers) that we can point in your direction.

A few firms I mentioned last week with smart thinkers about infrastructure for/by/around agents include folks like Race Capital, Category Ventures, and Sunflower; all are thoughtful early partners. (There are others too). There are also some folks we collaborate with regularly like Osage University Partners that are particularly thoughtful and helpful in terms of stewarding folks from academia through founder journeys or with company building. If any of these, or other specific folks you are talking with or seeking to talk with, sound interesting and we can help, please let me know.

Sorry that this doesn't represent an opportunity to work more closely together in an investment capacity but, I do hope to follow your journey and be of help to you in other ways if I can. Please let me know if these or other forms of advice would be of help.

Thank you,

Sam

threads.subject: Re: still working on details of a few slides

messages.subject: still working on details of a few slides
messages.body:
Don't over think it!  I'm around all sunday afternoon if you want feedback.

I really think that the interface in double agents and the process of
designing to show the right amount of coordination will resonate a lot.
This is because people are testing and questioning the value of agents, and
KNOW that it needs human in the loop solutions, but don't know what that
means.  I suggest leaning on double agents example.

I actually think the Akify slides detract from the message, because it's
selling a solution, which is 1) less believable to this audience, and 2) if
it's solved then there's no need for research. What Akify stands for as
future potential to motivate the rest of the topics is strong.

Here's a possible sequence

   - Rubik's cube stuff
   - Handling exceptions is the BIG automation challenge and it is a big
   HCI challenge
   - Simple example: seminar scheduling
   - challenges, power dynamics, etc.   people are full of exceptions.  S9
   is great
   - a way to convey how complex it is for the organizer,  example of
   something that *potentially* could be automated but details on when it
   could be trusted and could not be
   - challenge: extract policies (just show one or two)
      - tracking the automation and the state of the task is hard,
   particularly because agents are running in the back ground
   - challenge: interfaces need to [show the state of …. ?].
      - <Show interface and explain it>
      - It works!
   - this was just messages, we're not executing anything, calling any
   tools, etc.
   - Looking to the future and challenges:
   - Other knowledge sources
      - tools → ability to simulate, safety
      - cost, efficiency
      - other challenges…

threads.subject: Re: Intro to Akify team?

messages.subject: Intro to Akify team?
messages.body:
Hey Soya and Lydia, great to meet you and Lydia earlier this week and I wanted to get back quickly since I know you're in process. I think the space is super interesting overall and that the two of you have very impressive backgrounds but I'm falling short of the round here. I think it's a highly competitive space and I think there are interesting potentially prosumer ways to test the product out - and I'd want to understand that a bit more before leaning in. Really appreciate your time and excited to hear how things turn out with the fundraise!

Casey

Casey Aylward | Accel
Assistant: Kelly McDonald| @Accel

messages.subject: New messages from soya and Akify-dev in Akify
messages.body:
Hi Lydia,

You have a new direct message and a new mention from the Akify workspace (https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://akifyworkspace.slack.com/x-p9461450108277-9478301631393-9759967155779/team__;!!BDUfV1Et5lrpZQ!T-BlqxBQFpJMwSpxaFDn_nOvPcO1i_m7A5x0k5O4XrdrvMomzw7mnNg9l02tTrnVKIZUDHsYcgJ9-s86SEAsr5jlQsU$ ).

---

@soya
View in the archives: https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://akifyworkspace.slack.com/x-p9461450108277-9478301631393-9759967155779/archives/D09E28VN2QH/p1761333061924359__;!!BDUfV1Et5lrpZQ!T-BlqxBQFpJMwSpxaFDn_nOvPcO1i_m7A5x0k5O4XrdrvMomzw7mnNg9l02tTrnVKIZUDHsYcgJ9-s86SEAsL_mN2gY$ 

soya (3:11 PM, October 24th)

Hi Lydia, I wouldn't say event planning during investor meetings. I would rather talk about my work in IBM. It was fine during the meeting with Justin but just want to bring this up because I notice you said this before at other investor meetings :slightly_smiling_face: It is better for us to do IBM 1) I study about software workflow, so it shows we have domain expertise  2) it was actual full time job -- event planning sounds we randomly came across use case 3) we did get comment from investors (e.g. Cota captial) that "event planning" is not attractive.

I’ve mentioned this before, but I still don’t think onboarding fits our use case. While onboarding is common at many companies, it’s not something they typically pay for — which is why our competitor lists it as just one of many use cases. Positioning onboarding as our primary use case would actually weaken our company’s positioning. I’d prefer to focus on the use cases outlined in our Use Cases document. Also, onboarding isn’t included in our Investor Distribution document. And I think on-boarding has never been brought up when we talk to our design partners.

For the meetings we have today, I want to lead the use cases questions. Just reminder of the email I sent on 22th :slightly_smiling_face:

threads.subject: Re: [External Sender] Introduction to Capital One Ventures?

messages.subject: [External Sender] Introduction to Capital One Ventures?
messages.body:
Yay! This is the blurb we used for Bill's introduction. Feel free to edit
before send or I can help you co-edit the blurb too!  Let me know how I can
help!

Akify's blurb:

They’re working on enterprise AI that learns a company’s processes, keeps
them up to date, and executes them automatically. Based on their
background, expertise, and experience, they recognize that a company’s true
process knowledge lives in its employees — not just in data or code. Unlike
competitors, their human-in-the-loop technology captures these critical
knowledge gaps, then proactively automates the work once the gaps are
filled. They have foundational technology from their PhD and industry
experience; Soya earned her PhD at MIT CSAIL and previously built
enterprise productivity and collaboration systems. Lydia was a CS professor
at Columbia University (indefinitely on leave) and is a world-class expert
in AI workflow. Their research has been used in production at Google, IBM,
Amazon and Microsoft.

Their design partner is already live on the platform, and they recently
signed a $100K deal with Capital One. They just presented at SF Tech Week
and are seeing strong inbound interest from investors. They are already
backed by the VP of AI at AMD and other angels.

messages.subject: Introducing Vijay <> Soya and Lydia
messages.body:
Hi Vijay,

Hope your week goes well! Following up on our conversation Tuesday — I sent
over the materials that evening and wanted to check in on next steps.

You mentioned setting up an additional meeting; most of our tomorrow has
filled up, but we still have at or after 4pm if that works on your end.
Otherwise, happy to coordinate something next week.

Thanks,
Soya

Somewhat unrelated, but I wanted to extend an invitation to an event in NYC. (I saw the hanwha.nyc<https://urldefense.com/v3/__http://hanwha.nyc__;!!BDUfV1Et5lrpZQ!RstQRurJXqkiPg8C7IkVelRNUfKmVL1NQXPCVLy9zVtQY_tTbw4yEtx6VC6gm4GAB689WHzLshMU7AF4s17bqWUc11E$ > email address).

Columbia is running a workshop Monday 10/27 about "AI Agents at Work". We have keynotes from Anthropic and Cursor. It should be ~200 industry experts in AI and 20-30 Columbia faculty. I'll be speaking about Akify. It was very well attended when we did the first one in March.

Feel free to share the website, which contains the registration link, with people in NYC if it's of interest to them.

https://daplab.cs.columbia.edu/workshop/2025-10/ 

No worries if it doesn't work out - just thought it could be fun,
Lydia and Soya

threads.subject: Re: Some Notes from the Workshop and Partner Meetings

messages.subject: Some Notes from the Workshop and Partner Meetings
messages.body:
2 workshops is too much.   1 per year is probably reasonable — there's some
outward facing work we need to do.

I agree about partner retreats.  It should be partners presenting as well.

Infosys is already committed through SEAS, so we can work with them to
craft a research agenda aligned with the lab.

I'll follow up with the lab faculty list to get more notes from everyone.

On Wed, Oct 29, 2025 at 11:24 AM, Zhou Yu <zy2461@columbia.edu> wrote:

> Hi Eugene and Lydia,
>
> The workshop and partner meetings are a huge success. I would love to
> compare notes and see how we can improve for future events.
>
> 1: No workshop, but more industry partner meetings
> Lydia and I both think organizing a huge workshop event with 200-300
> people is too much work. The partner meetings are more beneficial to both
> sides. The students also benefit from listening to other faculty members.
> People attending both days get too tired on both the industry and the
> student side.
>
> 2: Ask industry partners to give talks as well in partner meetings
> For example, Infosys has a lot of agent applications that they have done.
> I think having people like Sivan speak would be very helpful. He has
> very good insights!
>
> Some learnings I have:
> Evaluation is the biggest bottleneck! Benchmark will be helpful to have.
> IBM's Agata was also talking about a multi-lingual open source benchmark
> for LLM training.
> Many partners and potential partners, such as Intellect, TD Bank etc are
> hiring good students; maybe we should do a DAPlab career fair at some point
> to do match-making to amplify that our partnership has strategic talent
> benefits. We can say xx students are hired by partners. which might be a
> compelling quote.
>
>
> -Jo
>

messages.subject: Long time… would love to reconnect and chat about your new company
messages.body:
Hey Lydia,

Very much looking forward to chatting with you & Soya! Unfortunately, we
weren't able to attend the event on Monday but we hope it went well.

We have a conflict tomorrow at 12pm so are you both available half an hour
later at 12:30pm instead?

Otherwise, we are flexible on Friday between 11am-3pm depending on what
works best for you. I'll update the calendar invite and let me know when
you prefer.

Thanks!
Ash

threads.subject: Re: Great meeting you at the AI Agents for Work conference

messages.subject: Great meeting you at the AI Agents for Work conference
messages.body:
Thank you so much for sharing these! I’ll plan to check out the Nov 11
meetup at Lincoln Center. The DAPLab talks sound really interesting too;
the timing doesn’t work for me this semester, but I passed it along to some
students and will look out for other chances to stay connected.

Looking forward to seeing you soon!

Warmly,

Sandrine

wrote:

> The next meet up is Monday Nov 11, from 7-9pm in Lincoln Center. I think

Is today’s part of the workshop somewhere else?-JohnOn Oct 26, 2025, at 21:11, Zhou Yu <zy2461@columbia.edu> wrote:﻿Here is the schedule, your talk will be 11-12https://daplab.cs.columbia.edu/workshop/2025-10/On Sun, Oct 26, 2025 at 9:06 PM John Hewitt <jh5020@columbia.edu> wrote:Hi everyone!I'm looking at my calendar and I've got all of tomorrow marked down for the workshop. Do you still want a lightning talk? If so, what time? Just wondering about place/schedule. Happy to help out if I'm still useful!-JohnOn Mon, Oct 13, 2025 at 2:56 PM Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu> wrote:Perfect! Thank you!There’s free food at 12, in case that will tempt you to stay a little while longer:)On Mon, Oct 13, 2025 at 14:45 John Hewitt <jh5020@columbia.edu> wrote:Ok! I've put down 9-12.-JohnOn Mon, Oct 13, 2025 at 2:39 PM Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu> wrote:If you could do the morning session (9-12) that would be the best! 10-12 might be ok, too.On Mon, Oct 13, 2025 at 1:57 PM John Hewitt <jh5020@columbia.edu> wrote:Great; I've booked down Oct 27.For Oct 28, can you give a rough few hours where I might be most useful?-JohnOn Mon, Oct 13, 2025 at 10:11 AM Eugene Wu <ew2493@columbia.edu> wrote:Hi John,We've chatted about it a bit in person but wanted to invite you to DAPLab's late October workshops.   There are twoOct 27 public workshop: we are hoping you can give a 10-minute lightning talk in the morning.  There will be a bunch of folks from industry, so hoping to get them excited about columbia.  https://www.eventbrite.com/e/ai-agents-for-work-oct-2025-tickets-1636311284099?aff=oddtdtcreatorOct 28 DAPLab retreat:  this is a 9-2PM event where the industry partners and potential partner come and we share our research agenda (what we've done, and our plans for the next year).   We are also asking partners to come with their big problems, and will have round tables to dig into what they are.   Would love for you to just join in on the activities.  If you want to present, that's great too and we can chat about it.eugene

threads.subject: Quick notes on your slides today

messages.subject: Quick notes on your slides today
messages.body:
A few things to keep in mind

   - You are representing the DAPLab today!   So it's not "I did such and
   such" but "We" or "the lab" did!
   - Try to call out to earlier parts in the agenda to strengthen coherence
   - The goal of the slides is to get the partners to talk!

Thanks everyone for participating :)

threads.subject: Following up from Columbia “AI Agents for Work” — Letters to Lives: Agent × History × HCI

messages.subject: Following up from Columbia “AI Agents for Work” — Letters to Lives: Agent × History × HCI
messages.body:
Dear Prof. Chilton,

It was wonderful speaking with you at the AI Agents for Work workshop — I really enjoyed seeing the DAPLab and Columbia CS posters (especially DoubleAgents, MindfulAgents, and JumpStarter) exploring transparency, trust, and proactive AI. They deeply resonated with what I’m building from a humanities and history perspective.

After graduating from GS at the end of 2024 with a degree in Psyc & CS (minor in Anthropology), I’m currently a Global Research & Impact Fellow at the International Institute of Rural Reconstruction (IIRR), writing a book and designing a digital archive on Dr. Y.C. James Yen and Alice Huie Yen—a Barnard alumna and one of China’s early rural reformers. Dr. Yen’s network included Eleanor Roosevelt, Pearl S. Buck, and Nelson Rockefeller, and Teachers College recently hosted a symposium honoring his legacy.

My project, Letters to Lives: Agent × History × HCI, aims to:

Digitize and transcribe letters and diaries from IIRR’s archives.
Train source-citing conversational agents that can answer questions by directly referencing primary materials.
Integrate a gender lens and a “Time-Machine” dialogue mode, so users can explore evolving ideas, relationships, and life choices across time—while distinguishing clearly between quotation and interpretation.

This system builds on many principles shared in your group’s work—especially human-in-the-loop trust design, provenance, and reflective interaction.

We would love your help on the following: 

Share the attached 1-pager and student team brief with HCI/IR/ML students who might collaborate on scanning, OCR, RAG, or UX.
Recommend suitable tools or frameworks for long-context retrieval and handwriting OCR (we’re testing Memories.ai, Transkribus, and eScriptorium).
Explore collaboration with Columbia’s History Department and your lab to co-teach a small module on AI × archives × design ethics.

Would you be open to a short meeting to discuss potential intersections?

I’d love to hear your thoughts on bridging historical archives and proactive AI design.

Thank you so much! 

Warm regards,

Ruoqi Amy Zhou 若祺

Global Research & Impact Fellow, IIRR

LinkedIn: LinkedIn.com/in/amy-ruoqi-zhou

messages.body:

Regards,
Sivan Veera
AVP Senior Principal
Specialist Technology Group
Infosys

Sent via the Samsung Galaxy S22 Ultra 5G, an AT&T 5G smartphone
Get Outlook for Android<https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://aka.ms/AAb9ysg__;!!BDUfV1Et5lrpZQ!Xq_VXkDv-xE6LPyXcqIBdN0zv3HO9qt0iw948c1frOvCEc3X1RKPPuxRsUfRYdcLSab-v25zXo5ooGF6wTAniBXgzxygzoi4$ >
________________________________
From: Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu>
Sent: Monday, October 27, 2025 9:47:43 AM
To: Sivan Veera <Kumarasivan.V01@infosys.com>
Subject: Fwd: DAPlab Workshop Industry Panel

[**EXTERNAL EMAIL**] Please verify sender address and exercise caution before clicking on any link.

Hi Sivan,

Here's the list of questions for the panel if you want to prepare :)

cheers,
lydia

---------- Forwarded message ---------
From: Zhou Yu <zy2461@columbia.edu<mailto:zy2461@columbia.edu>>
Date: Wed, Oct 22, 2025 at 11:57 AM
Subject: DAPlab Workshop Industry Panel
To: Diane Yu <diane@tidalwave.com<mailto:diane@tidalwave.com>>, Rotem Weiss <rotem@tavily.com<mailto:rotem@tavily.com>>, Zachary Chase Lipton <zlipton@cmu.edu<mailto:zlipton@cmu.edu>>, Joshua Fontanilla <joshua@abridge.com<mailto:joshua@abridge.com>>, Natalia Dorogi <Natalia_Dorogi@mckinsey.com<mailto:Natalia_Dorogi@mckinsey.com>>, <eric.h.kuang@gmail.com<mailto:eric.h.kuang@gmail.com>>, Deepak Dastrala <deepak.dastrala@intellectdesign.com<mailto:deepak.dastrala@intellectdesign.com>>
Cc: Eugene Wu <ew2493@columbia.edu<mailto:ew2493@columbia.edu>>, Lydia B. Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu<mailto:chilton@cs.columbia.edu>>

Hi All

Thanks again for attending panels and sharing your valuable insights on AI Agents adoptions in industry. Here are some example questions I will ask. We will open up to the audience.

1: What are some real deployed AI agent use cases that you have seen that have positive ROI?
2: What are the major bottlenecks in current AI Agent development?
3: What technology breakthrough in AI agent that is critical to obtain the dream-agent you want?
4: What metrics should we have to evaluate if an AI agent is successful, both quality and business metrics.
5: What are some contrarian findings you have in AI agent building or deployment processes?

Please let me know if you have any questions you would like me to ask, so you can share the insights that would be most meaningful.

Thanks!
-jo

Best Wishes
Founder of Arklex.ai & Computer Science Professor, Columbia University
Zhou(Jo) Yu
https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://www.linkedin.com/in/zhou-jo-yu-95327378/__;!!BDUfV1Et5lrpZQ!Xq_VXkDv-xE6LPyXcqIBdN0zv3HO9qt0iw948c1frOvCEc3X1RKPPuxRsUfRYdcLSab-v25zXo5ooGF6wTAniBXgz6Fs_mso$ <https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://www.linkedin.com/in/zhou-jo-yu-95327378/__;!!BDUfV1Et5lrpZQ!VePx7BWpHNecSJM8bC-X8iXOK3_8r-bFLaKKSUpP0s87LGullpZauorpkQHW5U-tRW6OvoyTpsDO70rl9NDMm2NL$>

threads.subject: AgentDynEx Poster

messages.subject: AgentDynEx Poster
messages.body:
Hi Lydia,

Should I present AgentDynEx instead as a poster for the DAPLab?

threads.subject: Question about Poster for DAPLab

messages.subject: Question about Poster for DAPLab
messages.body:
Hi Lydia,

I'm working on my poster for the DAPLab event next week and had a quick
question.

I'm not sure if Jenny is already planning to present a poster on AgentDynEx
- I've reached out to her but haven't heard back yet. I also recently saw
that the author order was updated in the CHI resubmission, which I wasn't
aware of beforehand, so I wanted to confirm whether you'd prefer that I
still present AgentDynEx or pivot to share my ongoing project (Rasoi),
which is also at a stage where feedback could be helpful.

Thanks!

threads.subject: UI/UX Opportunities for Spring 2026

messages.subject: UI/UX Opportunities for Spring 2026
messages.body:
Hi Professor Chilton,

I took UI Design with you in Spring 2025 and was very interested in the Humor Project this semester. However, I’m currently on a gap semester for a Software Engineering Internship, so I wasn’t able to participate. I wanted to ask if there might be any similar opportunities to the Humor Project next semester (Spring 2026).

I’m very interested in UI/UX design, and since there aren’t many courses in this area at Columbia, I’d love the chance to get involved in projects like yours if possible.

Please let me know.

Thank you,
Anna Sun

Hi Professor Chilton,

I hope you had a great weekend!

I am looking to get *approval* to use *COMS 6901* (*CBL/Humor Project* in
the summer semester) towards my *secondary 6000-level elective*. I am a
*MSCS* student on the *Software Systems track*.

Professor Stroustrup (my advisor) mentioned that he will approve it if you
do.

Please let me know if you need any additional materials from me. Thank you!

Best,
Celine

threads.subject: Joining the Humor Project

messages.subject: Joining the Humor Project
messages.body:
Dear Professor Chilton,

I hope this email finds you well! I realize the fourth week of the semester
is already here but I wanted to ask if there might still be a chance to
join the Humor Project class. I took UI Design last semester and really
enjoyed it :)

I’m currently planning to drop another course, which will put me at 11
credits. Since I need 12 credits to maintain full-time student status, I
was hoping this could be a good fit both academically and practically. I
completely understand if it’s too late to add, but I thought I’d check just
in case!

Best,
Maria Janoo, maj2198

>>> wrote:
>>>
>>>> I approve.
>>>>
>>>> (this should be all the proof you need to upload).
>>>>
>>>> signed RAF is attached. I couldn't get the PDF reader to put my uni in,
>>>> but hopefully it's okay - it's the same as the one able.
>>>>
>>>> cheers,
>>>> lydia
>>>>
>>>> On Tue, May 27, 2025 at 9:37 PM Akshita Lathar <al4848@columbia.edu>
>>>> wrote:
>>>>
>>>>> Hello Professor Lydia,
>>>>>
>>>>> I am a CVN student and in order to register for the COMS 6901 (Humor
>>>>> Project) I need to fill out a form that requires 2 attachments. First is
>>>>> the proof that the instructor approved my enrollment in the course and
>>>>> second is the RAF form. I have attached the RAF form which requires your
>>>>> signature.
>>>>> I am not sure what proof I can upload for approval so I would
>>>>> appreciate any help there.
>>>>>
>>>>> Thank you
>>>>> Akshita
>>>>>
>>>>

threads.subject: Request to Access Summer Course Grades

messages.subject: Request to Access Summer Course Grades
messages.body:
Hello Professor Chilton,

I hope this message finds you well. I attended CBL and Humor Project
courses in the summer but deferred them to the Fall semester. I noticed
that grades for the summer have now been released, but since my enrollment
was deferred, I’m unable to see my grade.

If possible, I would greatly appreciate it if you could let me know my
grade, so I at least have an idea of my performance.

Thanks,
Maddison Hoveida

threads.subject: Kendra scanning is complete!

messages.subject: Kendra scanning is complete!
messages.body:
After multiple attempts and security tweaking I completed the scanning :)

I aborted the scanning as it was scanning your entire gmail. I re-synced
and filtered only files under the Humor project. 197 files  failed because
their file types are images or zips.

[image: image.png]

threads.subject: Re: Friday, September 5th

messages.subject: Friday, September 5th
messages.body:
:)

________________________________
From: Michael Roger <michael.roger@columbia.edu>
Sent: Wednesday, August 27, 2025 11:59:06 AM
To: Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu>
Subject: Re: Friday, September 5th

Resending to confirm.

Do you mind if Bradley attends our first humor project meeting with Weijie and Renee on Friday, September 5th?

Thanks!
MR

On Mon, Aug 18, 2025 at 10:57 AM Michael Roger <mr4217@columbia.edu<mailto:mr4217@columbia.edu>> wrote:

Last question:  Bradley’s parents will hand off Bradley to Weijie and Renee for the afternoon on that day.

Our Humor Meeting is from 4pm to 5pm.  Are you okay with Bradley attending that meeting with them?

MR

From: Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu<mailto:chilton@cs.columbia.edu>>
Date: Monday, August 18, 2025 at 10:29 AM
To: Michael Roger <mr4217@columbia.edu<mailto:mr4217@columbia.edu>>
Subject: Re: Friday, September 5th

ok, as long as we don't talk about MIT. Would we do 10-11 that day? Or 10:30 if that's better for you all?

On Mon, Aug 18, 2025 at 9:18 AM Michael Roger <mr4217@columbia.edu<mailto:mr4217@columbia.edu>> wrote:

Oh!  I was just excited to have you meet them.  I talk about you with them so much (and vice versa) that it seems good to have everyone meet.

I would say the conversation wouldn’t just be about MIT, but your educational experience as a whole.

This would also include your decision to get a PhD and your life as a Professor.

Weijie and Renee are taking Bradley to Harvard and MIT in October for an insider tour with their friends there, so your information about MIT wouldn’t be the only data point for Bradley.

MR

From: Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu<mailto:chilton@cs.columbia.edu>>
Date: Monday, August 18, 2025 at 8:11 AM
To: Michael Roger <mr4217@columbia.edu<mailto:mr4217@columbia.edu>>
Subject: Re: Friday, September 5th

I’ve been thinking about this… I really don’t have anything good to say about MIT. But that was my experience. And it was 20 years ago. I don’t want to color their thoughts with my personal stuff that may or may not be representative of what the current experience is.

I could talk about other things maybe?

On Mon, Aug 18, 2025 at 15:05 Michael Roger <mr4217@columbia.edu<mailto:mr4217@columbia.edu>> wrote:

We could do a different time if 11am doesn’t work!

MR

From: Michael Roger <mr4217@columbia.edu<mailto:mr4217@columbia.edu>>
Date: Friday, August 15, 2025 at 8:46 AM
To: Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu<mailto:chilton@cs.columbia.edu>>
Subject: Friday, September 5th

Hey Professor!

Do you have time to meet with Bradley and his parents and me on Friday, September 5th at 11am?

He has a bunch of MIT questions to ask you.

Thanks!

MR

threads.subject: Specialization Track

messages.subject: Specialization Track
messages.body:
Hello Professor,

I am a Graduate student in the CVN program, and my first class at Columbia
was your UI Design class. I also worked on the Humor project over the
summer and plan to continue it in the Fall.
As I move forward in my Master's, I am hoping to focus on UI design, HCI,
and frontend-related areas. Since Columbia does not have a formal track for
this, I would like to create my own, which requires a faculty advisor.
Given my strong interest in your area and a really good learning experience
in the UI class, I was wondering if you might be willing to be my track
advisor.

I completely understand the demands on your time, but I would be very
grateful if you would consider it. Please let me know if you have any
questions or concerns.
Thank you very much for your time and consideration.

Akshita

threads.subject: Re: COMS9911E060 Waitlist

messages.subject: COMS9911E060 Waitlist
messages.body:
Hey Xinyi!

Could you email me a copy of your resume?

The Humor Project class is invitation only.

MR

________________________________
From: Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu>
Sent: Friday, August 15, 2025 1:23 PM
To: Michael Roger <michael.roger@columbia.edu>
Subject: Fwd: COMS9911E060 Waitlist

looks like a lost duck :)

---------- Forwarded message ---------
From: Xinyi Gui <xg2426@barnard.edu<mailto:xg2426@barnard.edu>>
Date: Fri, Aug 15, 2025 at 12:02 AM
Subject: Re: COMS9911E060 Waitlist
To: Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu<mailto:chilton@cs.columbia.edu>>

This Message Is From an External Sender
This message came from outside your organization.

Hi,

Yeas. I am looking for the humor project, and I really want to join this course. But at the beginning, the course number didn’t confirm, so I enrolled COMS9911. Could you please tell me what can I do next step?

Best,
Xinyi

On Fri, Aug 15, 2025 at 5:16 AM Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu<mailto:chilton@cs.columbia.edu>> wrote:
Hi, I see you're on the waitlist for COMS9911E060. What project were you trying to sign up for for? That is probably the wrong course number.

threads.subject: Re: Request to Advise for MS Personalized Track

messages.subject: Request to Advise for MS Personalized Track
messages.body:
Aw ok! See you in September.

Also I heard you're teaching a special section of CC, that's super
exciting...

Just to give a preview, these are the courses I was planning on taking for
the MS (the greyed out ones are the ones I took during undergrad!)

Operating Systems (COMS 4118)
Algorithms (CSOR 4231)
Machine Learning (COMS 4771)
Computer Networks or John Hewitt's NLP
Engineering Software as a Service (COMS 4152)
Spoken Language Processing (COMS 6998)
Design for Gen AI (COMS 6998)
Humor Project (COMS 6901)
Agentic Systems Made Real (COMS E6113)
Innovation and Design Lab (COMS 4995)

Best regards,
Aaron

On Tue, Aug 5, 2025 at 4:59 AM Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu>
wrote:

> yeah! let's do it :)
>
> I'm not in NYC next week, but let's catch up in september.
>
> On Tue, Aug 5, 2025 at 3:25 AM Aaron Zhu <azz2111@columbia.edu> wrote:
>
>> Hi Professor Chilton,
>>
>> Hope everything's been well with you this summer! Kent expressed his
>> excitement to me today when he found out you worked just one floor above
>> him, in JFK27 — small world! If you’ll be in the office next week, I might
>> be flying back to New York and would love to catch up if you're around.
>>
>> In other news, I’m starting the MSCS at Columbia this fall and planning
>> to pursue the Personalized Track. I wanted to ask if you’d be open to being
>> my advisor for it. I’ve already completed 5 out of the 10 required courses,
>> including The Humor Project and Design for Gen AI with you, as well as
>> Innovation and Design for Gen AI with Professor Zamchick. Those courses —
>> and of course taking and TAing UI Design — helped me significantly more
>> intentional and methodical when it comes to building technology for people.
>>
>> Now I’m hoping to round things out with stronger fundamentals in some of
>> the more technical aspects of systems and AI. Namely, I would like to get
>> more comfortable modeling problems using the frameworks and tools from
>> these areas.
>>
>> I’m reaching out because I feel you’d understand the kind of balance I’m
>> going for — combining practical, human-centered design with a solid
>> grounding in the underlying technical ideas. A little more long term, my
>> plan is to hang around in big tech for a bit, then sooner rather than
>> later, start a startup of my own.
>>
>> I’d really appreciate your support as I plan my next phase in this
>> journey!
>>
>> Best regards,
>> Aaron
>>
>

threads.subject: Re: CBL Question

messages.subject: CBL Question
messages.body:
Yes, it’s clear. Thank you so much for your help!

> On Jul 31, 2025, at 12:10 PM, Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu> wrote:
> 
> Same link :) you could just register for a different number, like 3998 then 4998 or 6901. They’re all the same basically… but you need to register for different numbers for humor and CBL. Is that kinda clear?
> 
> On Thu, Jul 31, 2025 at 12:05 Maddison Hoveida <mh4572@columbia.edu <mailto:mh4572@columbia.edu>> wrote:
>> Thank you for your response. For the Humor Project, I received a registration link directly from Michael, so I can see it in Vergil. However, for CBL, I haven’t received any link, and I couldn’t find the course listed in Vergil. I’d appreciate any guidance on how to proceed.
>> 
>>> On Jul 31, 2025, at 11:38 AM, Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu <mailto:chilton@cs.columbia.edu>> wrote:
>>> 
>>> Aug 4 is fine :)
>>> 
>>> On Thu, Jul 31, 2025 at 11:37 Maddison Hoveida <mh4572@columbia.edu <mailto:mh4572@columbia.edu>> wrote:
>>>> Hi Professor Chilton,
>>>> 
>>>> I hope you're doing well! I’m not sure if you’re the right person to ask this question, but I’ve already emailed lmb2321@gsb.columbia.edu <mailto:lmb2321@gsb.columbia.edu> and cbl@gsb.columbia.edu <mailto:cbl@gsb.columbia.edu> and haven’t heard back. I’m currently attending CBL, but wanted to defer my credits to the Fall 2025 semester. Since my first registration appointment is on August 4th, I wanted to ask if it’s okay to register on that day, or do I need to register earlier? If earlier registration is required, how can I do that, given that the system doesn’t allow me to register before August 4th? If I should contact another email address, please let me know.
>>>> 
>>>> Thanks,
>>>> Maddison Hoveida
>>>> 
>>

messages.body:
Let’s do 10:30am. Looking forward to it.

Lance Weiler
Associate Professor Film & Theatre
Founding Director
Columbia University SOA Digital Storytelling Lab
https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=http-3A__digitalstorytellinglab.com&d=DwIFaQ&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=hPTJFKhz3Hkwikm3mM2701VB1AZoIWoRMgOYWA3SHD0&m=tZkJVVutkyLwrKhbGIzUvFA6bspi6pOW_5feTP47hyXsAwIcOHElUGlqLcvK8VoF&s=hXzyafq_vVr_OMBtR_a0FrYZ627JHe9_fy5uEUBLElg&e= 

On Sat, Jul 26, 2025 at 5:33 PM Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu>
wrote:

> I'm in Europe (+6 hours), but 11am NYC time tomorrow would be great for
> me! If could do a little earlier - like 10:30 that would be slightly
> better. But if not, no worries, we can keep it to 30 minutes - something is
> always better than nothing. It at least lets me know how to redirect my
> energies :)
>
> On Sat, Jul 26, 2025 at 5:06 PM lance.weiler@columbia.edu <
> lance.weiler@columbia.edu> wrote:
>
>> What tomorrow morning at 11am like for you? Or I can talk around 6ish
>> today?
>>
>>
>>
>> Lance Weiler
>> Associate Professor Film & Theatre
>> Founding Director
>> Columbia University SOA Digital Storytelling Lab
>> https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=http-3A__digitalstorytellinglab.com&d=DwIFaQ&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=hPTJFKhz3Hkwikm3mM2701VB1AZoIWoRMgOYWA3SHD0&m=tZkJVVutkyLwrKhbGIzUvFA6bspi6pOW_5feTP47hyXsAwIcOHElUGlqLcvK8VoF&s=hXzyafq_vVr_OMBtR_a0FrYZ627JHe9_fy5uEUBLElg&e= 
>> <https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=http-3A__digitalstorytellinglab.com&d=DwMFaQ&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=hPTJFKhz3Hkwikm3mM2701VB1AZoIWoRMgOYWA3SHD0&m=6nuvvcEOhVMYk6rTGkNY0q0JLp2M5Kcipn0d0bkapEWtI2kqw8-CHFuOkvhujyeY&s=erBaXgLScSGLNkPvKFBa5WL7IV-V_bLEhbOFoKJ1MHo&e=>
>>
>>
>> On Sat, Jul 26, 2025 at 4:29 PM Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu>
>> wrote:
>>
>>> awesome! I can get on a call and get you set up with that. Even now :)
>>>
>>> On Sat, Jul 26, 2025 at 4:24 PM lance.weiler@columbia.edu <
>>> lance.weiler@columbia.edu> wrote:
>>>
>>>> Hi Lydia,
>>>>
>>>> Hope all is well. I’m back from our trip. I had a chance to test the
>>>> bot. My head is swimming with possibilities. Is there a way from me to
>>>> remix it? Meaning can I contribute to the gpt and what are you using to
>>>> manipulate the page? JavaScript?
>>>>
>>>> Thanks
>>>> Lance
>>>>
>>>> Lance Weiler
>>>> Associate Professor Film & Theatre
>>>> Founding Director
>>>> Columbia University SOA Digital Storytelling Lab
>>>> https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=http-3A__digitalstorytellinglab.com&d=DwIFaQ&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=hPTJFKhz3Hkwikm3mM2701VB1AZoIWoRMgOYWA3SHD0&m=tZkJVVutkyLwrKhbGIzUvFA6bspi6pOW_5feTP47hyXsAwIcOHElUGlqLcvK8VoF&s=hXzyafq_vVr_OMBtR_a0FrYZ627JHe9_fy5uEUBLElg&e= 
>>>> <https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=http-3A__digitalstorytellinglab.com&d=DwMFaQ&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=hPTJFKhz3Hkwikm3mM2701VB1AZoIWoRMgOYWA3SHD0&m=vBSp5u9dwOeA5IYPjvk0Ok4cSbjFFLR8pEZuNiSszMkPfHyGtWBu9vaOIiYErHex&s=3Xoi1S6SX6EaKLdl4xWHVgZo3GiVgyNLYyN8E8Navzw&e=>
>>>>
>>>>
>>>> On Thu, Jul 24, 2025 at 7:49 AM Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu>
>>>> wrote:
>>>>
>>>>> I know you're still touring, but I wanted to drop a link:
>>>>>
>>>>>
>>>>> https://www.cs.columbia.edu/~chilton/projects/human_or_bot_chat_decoration/ 
>>>>>
>>>>> You can see some of the ways we can extend the chat. whenever you have
>>>>> 30 seconds, chat with it for 4 or 5 turns and you can see demos of very
>>>>> simple things that are easy to do (now that I know how). and we could do
>>>>> much more - and definitely subvert expectations :)
>>>>>
>>>>> On Wed, Jul 23, 2025 at 5:00 PM Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu>
>>>>> wrote:
>>>>>
>>>>>> No worries at all :) at your own pace - I just wanna make sure I keep
>>>>>> my part going :)
>>>>>>
>>>>>> My flight lands 1pm Monday, so I should definitely make it to the DSL
>>>>>> event in plenty of time. I’ll bring some

messages.subject: Fwd: [CHI 2026] Decision for paper 7303 – not accepted
messages.body:
bummer ---

---------- Forwarded message ---------
From: Alessandro Bozzon, Thomas Kosch, Q. Vera Liao, Xiaojuan Ma <
chi26c@precisionconference.com>
Date: Tue, Nov 4, 2025 at 8:17 PM
Subject: [CHI 2026] Decision for paper 7303 – not accepted
To: Tao Long <long@cs.columbia.edu>

Dear Tao Long, Unfortunately, your paper: DoubleAgents: Exploring
Mechanisms of Building Trust with Proactive AI (number 7303) has not been
accepted to CHI 2026 Papers. All papers that met a minimum threshold - at
least one committee member
ZjQcmQRYFpfptBannerStart
This Message Is From an External Sender
This message came from outside your organization.

ZjQcmQRYFpfptBannerEnd

Dear Tao Long,

Unfortunately, your paper: DoubleAgents: Exploring Mechanisms of
Building Trust with Proactive AI (number 7303) has not been accepted
to CHI 2026 Papers.

All papers that met a minimum threshold - at least one committee
member (1AC, 2AC) recommending: RR, Accept or RR, or Accept - have
been put through to round 2.

Any paper that did not receive at least one of these recommendations
did not meet the threshold for entering round 2. This typically means
that, although there may be merit in this paper, reviewers thought it
could not be sufficiently improved within the four-week
revise-and-resubmit period. Your reviews are below, and will also be
shown in PCS soon.

In a few rare cases, papers were found to meet the desk rejection
criteria later in the review process; if your paper was one of these,
you may have fewer than normal reviews and/or a comment to the author
instead of reviews.

If you are interested in future publication, you could consider
whether a version of the work is suitable for another track at CHI
2026 (https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://chi2026.acm.org/authors/__;!!BDUfV1Et5lrpZQ!W_s1k7EyWXHtrIlDYOCr1LqQES79FzCtJlqBerJ-X2tiAQpn4E-jN5BFv2OcHb24Unm4NLrcBAtXQDncKDqfxGJEsOg$)
or submitting to next year's CHI 2026 (or another conference) after
making substantial changes to your paper.

We wish you the best of luck with future submissions relating to this work.

Alessandro Bozzon, Thomas Kosch, Q. Vera Liao, Xiaojuan Ma
CHI 2026 Papers Chairs

Reviews:
----------------------------------------------------------------

1AC review (reviewer 4)

  Expertise

    Passing Knowledge

  Originality (Round 1)

    Medium originality

  Significance (Round 1)

    Low significance

  Research Quality (Round 1)

    Low research quality

  Recommendation (Round 1)

    I can go with either Reject or Revise and Resubmit

  1AC: The Meta-Review

    The work proposes an interface for an agentic scheduling systems that uses
                simulated results as a way of building trust with users.
    The reviewers found the manuscript well written, although a bit
too long, and the
                topic is certainly timely. 2AC namely notes that
integrating simulated
                personas is an interesting way to unit test the system.

    However, the main weaknesses of the paper are that

    A/ the studies are methodologically weak:
            - undersized studies: only 2 participants in the
deployment evaluation, 10
                in the 2-day study
            - for the 2-day study, all participants saw the same
condition, without a
                baseline comparison. This can not justify the validity of the
                particular design choices
            - limited amount of details, namely regarding participant
recruitment
                (sampling method, skillset), which makes the results
hard to interpret

    B/ and does not build well / differentiate from existing work in
this space. The
                reviewers namely highlight that the authors should better:
            - justify how the design and interaction are motivated by
the literature
                on human AI trust
            - contrast the obtained results to the existing literature
on Human-AI
                trust and AI/LLM scheduling
            - contrast the system to exist

threads.subject: Interested in PhD in Computer Science under Your Supervision

messages.subject: Interested in PhD in Computer Science under Your Supervision
messages.body:
Dear Professor Chilton,

I hope this email finds you well. My name is Joydeep Chandra, currently
pursuing a Master’s in Advanced Computing at Tsinghua University, China,
and working as a researcher at the Beijing National Research Center for
Information Science and Technology, Tsinghua University, as well as a
Foreign Language LLM development intern at OPPO AI Center. I am writing to
express my strong interest in pursuing a PhD under your supervision at
Columbia University, focusing on the intersection of Human-Centered AI,
Generative Co-Creation Systems, and Multi-Agent Interaction for Creative
and Educational Support.

Your remarkable work on DoubleAgents, Simulating Cooperative Prosocial
Behavior with Multi-Agent LLMs, ReelFramer, PodReels, StoryWars, and
JumpStarter has been deeply inspiring. I am particularly drawn to your
research that explores creativity, collaboration, and human-AI co-design
through participatory and longitudinal HCI studies. The conceptual and
technical depth of your work on generative co-creation spanning domains
such as narrative design, creative media, and goal-oriented interaction
resonates strongly with my interest in designing ethical, adaptive, and
value-sensitive AI systems that amplify human creativity and learning.

My research interests: Human-Centered and Responsible AI, Teachable
Conversational Agents, Multimodal Multi-Agent Systems, Curiosity-Driven and
Reflective Learning, Value-Aligned Interactive Systems, Assistive AI for
Education and Social Good, and Ethical Interaction Design for Inclusive HCI.

My recent work includes:

*TrueGL (WebTrust) [Accepted in ICDM]:* Designed an AI-driven system to
assess the reliability of web search results on a 0–100 scale, using our
own curated custom-labeled dataset and fine-tuned IBM Granite-1B model on
it. Integrated plug-and-play reasoning modules to provide transparent,
interpretable truth assessments, enhancing user trust in online information.

*DAMDA (Distributed Adaptive Multimodal Data Agents) [Under submission to
SIGMOD and US patent]:* Developing a secure, privacy-preserving multimodal
dataspace architecture that enables inter-agent collaboration across
organizations while maintaining strong performance guarantees. The system
integrates fine-tuned LLMs for reasoning over heterogeneous data, ensuring
both compliance and accessibility.

*Ethical Multi-Agent Systems [Under submission to CHI]:* Building a
socially aligned multi-agent framework to support diverse user groups such
as children, neurodivergent individuals, and the elderly within adaptive AI
systems.

I am particularly excited about the possibility of exploring the following
under your guidance:

   1.

   Designing multi-agent co-creative systems that model trust, social
   alignment, and mutual adaptation in collaborative contexts, inspired by
   *DoubleAgents* and *Simulating Strategic Reasoning*.
   2.

   Developing interactive authoring and co-design tools that leverage
   generative AI to enhance creativity, storytelling, and learning while
   preserving user intent and authorship.

I would be grateful for the opportunity to collaborate on any of your
ongoing or future projects. While I do not have a fixed proposal at this
time, I am very open to co-developing one that aligns with your research
priorities and current lab directions. I am also open to any pre-doctoral
collaboration opportunities that could serve as a foundation for a formal
PhD application in the 2026 intake.

Please find my CV attached, and I would be happy to share additional
materials, references, or attend an interview at your convenience.

Thank you very much for your time and consideration!

Warm regards,
Joydeep Chandra

threads.subject: Peggy from Stanford HCI seminar

messages.subject: Peggy from Stanford HCI seminar
messages.body:
Hi Lydia!
I'm Peggy — the psychology PhD student who approached you after your talk on DoubleAgents for education and diplomacy. I'm advised by Carol Dweck, and my lab frequently does interventions that mediate between teacher-student mismatches in perception of goals, particularly in contexts where there's social stigma. Outside of research, I direct the Cognitive Security Task Force<https://urldefense.com/v3/__http://cstf.dev__;!!BDUfV1Et5lrpZQ!TP8gFWsBVLvlCIwosLwsteqDi2Rgl3YyVpCpJDp9JqpX6AaIMjIp0MtSZursSvaWgF68Y-ZHkE-uIF-MoKPF_bMq9KY$ > <https://urldefense.com/v3/__http://cstf.dev__;!!BDUfV1Et5lrpZQ!TP8gFWsBVLvlCIwosLwsteqDi2Rgl3YyVpCpJDp9JqpX6AaIMjIp0MtSZursSvaWgF68Y-ZHkE-uIF-MoKPF_bMq9KY$ > at HAI, where we're working on developing frameworks and prototypes that strengthen public trust and wellbeing at a time when cognitive warfare is becoming a hot-topic amongst military circles. I would love to speak with you on your current work, and ways to translate insights from DoubleAgents into social contexts.

Warmly,
Peggy

Dear Professor:

I hope this email finds you well!

I am Yuetong, and I worked in your lab with Tao on DoubleAgents during the summer. I mainly worked on simulating responses from the user side and handling edge cases. I am deeply grateful for the guidance and inspiration you provided, which not only sparked my interest in the field of Human-Computer Interaction but also fostered my research capabilities.

As I prepare to apply for the Master of Science in Computer Science at Columbia University and a few other schools this semester, I would be honored if you could write a letter of recommendation to support my application. I believe that your endorsement would greatly strengthen my candidacy, given your familiarity with my research interests.

I understand that the Fall term is a busy time for you, so I will send you a draft recommendation letter later this week. I have attached my CV and transcript for your reference, and you can also find some work I did during the summer in this Google Doc: https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://docs.google.com/document/d/18om6qzRAHepNFgB9sAZzcrR-dQXv-UiJ-Kgl-GOn2xI/edit?pli=1&tab=t.jhlj00hzdub3__;!!BDUfV1Et5lrpZQ!TngN_YiPriacXw-mdg1J8tIv5-E2cFbSvud5uCulkzj1zqwDJeoF6ixEnVMfuJJyshH_qG13yW_xOKJBecXldD4s$ <https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://docs.google.com/document/d/18om6qzRAHepNFgB9sAZzcrR-dQXv-UiJ-Kgl-GOn2xI/edit?pli=1&tab=t.jhlj00hzdub3__;!!BDUfV1Et5lrpZQ!TiLIXfRl9TXE0SKNIv1poXBjtuL87UND8eWT-MQjBgpeW9cDOEf1cGyUcVCFBVlTeRnKuVoOxfATnXpnNtXfryJG$>

Sincerely
Wu Yuetong

threads.subject: Re: Research interest - Calin-Andrei Puscalau, Egleston Scholar

messages.subject: Research interest - Calin-Andrei Puscalau, Egleston Scholar
messages.body:
Dear Professor Lydia Chilton,

I hope this message finds you well. I wanted to follow up on our previous
conversation regarding the research project “DoubleAgents: Using AI Agents
for Task Completion and Evaluation.”

At the great risk of annoying you, bothering you, distracting you from your
work, irritating you, upsetting you, stressing you out, and making you pick
up your phone and read this email saying, “Oh, what does this kid want now?”
 , I still remain very, very interested in your project.

As I mentioned in my previous emails, I want to get involved in this
research project primarily because I see it as a natural continuation of
the research I did back in high school. I love doing research — it's truly
a way for me to express myself.

The main reason I chose to come to the U.S., 10 hours away by direct flight
from Romania's capital and another 4 hours by car from my hometown, is
precisely because of people like you and the incredible work you do, work
that inspires for a better future. I chose to come to Columbia because it
is a powerhouse of research and innovation — an infinite book of knowledge.

I want to be part of that book. I want to be part of Columbia University's
story. I want to learn everything I can about your project and the
perspectives it offers. I would be more than happy to have the chance to
read more about it, to learn, to attend any office hour, and to try to
contribute in any way I can.

I'm ready to dedicate as much time as needed to this project (even if it
means having an extra cup of coffee! 😊).

Thank you very much!

Respectfully,

Calin-Andrei Puscalau

On Tue, Oct 7, 2025 at 9:50 PM Calin-Andrei Puscalau <cap2297@columbia.edu>
wrote:

> Dear Professor Lydia Chilton,
>
> Thank you for the follow up question ! Because I am interested in
> research at the intersection of AI and economics, I spoke with my advisor
> from the Egleston scholars program, Lilibeth Ramos Flores, and she told me
> about the partnership between Columbia Engineering and Capital One within
> the Center for AI and Responsible Financial Innovation. That’s how I first
> learned about your project on DoubleAgents.
>
> Respectfully,
> Calin-Andrei Puscalau
>
> On Tue, Oct 7, 2025 at 9:34 PM Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu>
> wrote:
>
>> quick follow up: I'm confused about how you heard about DoubleAgnts. did
>> you find it on arxiv? were you at the CapitalOne event? Did you talk to One
>> of my PhD students who work on it?
>>
>> On Tue, Oct 7, 2025 at 7:28 PM Calin-Andrei Puscalau <
>> cap2297@columbia.edu> wrote:
>>
>>> Dear Professor Lydia Chilton,
>>>
>>> Thank you for your questions !
>>>
>>> Why am I interested in AI DoubleAgents ?
>>>
>>> It is funny because I did not start my research journey with the initial
>>> desire to do research in AI, but I began to love research in this field
>>> when I noticed that AI is integrated into every cutting-edge research in
>>> all STEM fields. I was surprised to see that a large part of the work I had
>>> to perform during my internship at CERN in particle physics was based 70%
>>> on AI, more specifically ML. In short, the job of people at CERN is to see
>>> on a computer a particle resulting from the collision of two other
>>> particles. However, they collide 1 billion such particles in a nanosecond,
>>> thanks to the accelerator in Switzerland. From some events comes the
>>> particle we are interested in, and from others it does not. Then we sort
>>> them, and we do this with AI. Fun fact: if AI did not exist, and if
>>> researchers had started in the Late Cretaceous to sort “by hand” all the
>>> events from only one minute at CERN, they would not have finished even
>>> today. So I would say that working there developed my passion for AI, being
>>> amazed by its power, which I continued to pursue by working on the
>>> cybersecurity project at SAFETECH, where I used AI only in the Task
>>> Completion part (a single role).
>>>
>>> What I find truly impressive is this conc

messages.subject: 回复: Doubleagent
messages.body:
Dear Professor:
    I hope this email finds you well.
    I noticed the recently submitted paper on arXiv titled "DoubleAgents: Exploring Mechanisms of Building Trust with Proactive AI." In our previous email correspondence, you had assured me that I would be included as one of the authors. However, I see that my name is not listed on this article. As I am currently applying for Master's and PhD programs in the United States, the publication of this paper is very important to me. I would like to politely inquire whether there might have been some misunderstanding regarding this matter.
    Looking forward to your reply.
Sincerely
Wu Yuetong
________________________________

threads.subject: Interest in DoubleAgents Project and Center for AI and Responsible Financial Innovation

messages.subject: Interest in DoubleAgents Project and Center for AI and Responsible Financial Innovation
messages.body:
Dear Professor Chilton,

I hope this message finds you well. My name is Michelle Liu, and I am
currently a Master of Public Administration student at Columbia
University’s School of International and Public Affairs, focusing on
technology policy, AI, and venture innovation.

I recently came across your *DoubleAgents* project and was immediately
drawn to its approach to improving human-AI collaboration through task
coordination and evaluation. In particular, I am also very interested in
the broader work of the Center for AI and Responsible Financial Innovation,
and how it aligns with my academic and professional interests.

By way of background, I have experience in venture capital and growth
equity analysis across AI, robotics, and emerging technologies. I
previously worked with Plug and Play in Silicon Valley and Asia, as well as
with Gobi Partners and New York Robotics, where I conducted due diligence
on AI/robotics ventures, built valuation models, and explored
simulation-based evaluation frameworks. At Columbia, my research includes
AI ethics of robotics and AR/VR applications, and I also serve as a global
board member of the Columbia Venture Community.

Given my background and strong interest in human-AI coordination systems, I
would love to ask if there are any opportunities to get involved with the
*DoubleAgents* project in any capacity—whether through research assistance,
collaboration, or other forms of participation. I have attached my CV for
your reference.

Thank you very much for your time and consideration. I would be grateful
for the chance to learn more about your work and potentially contribute to
it.

Best regards,
Michelle Liu
ml5151@columbia.edu
--
*Ziyi Michelle Liu*

917-402-9957 | Columbia '26

https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__calendly.com_ziyimichelle_30min&d=DwIFaQ&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=-_LS5uo2o7-geJ5gnr0XLcB_sGrtzBYehOspkA3M8gk&m=wqiv3hjjANUuCn926LT3Gwy4XF0Hi4oYLk97POP9WekF0dIwzPZow-m0uAFUWKLK&s=3f2Aa8Q-Nz9m0ItjUMdEeMzmM9OumbeGWJqKFzlz0Kw&e=

threads.subject: Inquiry About Potential Research Projects

messages.subject: Inquiry About Potential Research Projects
messages.body:
Dear Professor Chilton,

My name is Kaan Erdem and I am an undergraduate Computer Science student
(SEAS '27). I was in your AI in Context class in the fall of last year and
became very interested in your work. Last year, I started meeting up with
Billy, and he told me about projects that you are involved in. We met to
discuss some of the papers and Billy kindly showed me more related papers,
which I've been exploring, to improve my understanding.

One of the projects Billy introduced me to was JumpStarter: I'm a big fan
of solving these kinds of issues through adaptive context curation. And
what particularly impressed me was the fact that JumpStarter was more
efficient than ChatGPT–I've always found noticeably outperforming base
models very challenging. So, the paper focuses on creating the plan and
initial drafts, and I'm curious about the journey that follows–when it
comes to actually taking action. I would find exploring that really
interesting. This is also somewhat related to what you suggested as
'future work' in your paper when you mentioned the motivational aspect that
people face when starting out with something. If you have any potential
follow-up ideas or projects exploring this space, I would love to
contribute.

I also read through your DynEx paper and found the Design Matrix approach
unique. I am also super interested in this work, and would love to
contribute in any capacity. In our most recent meeting, Billy also told me
about your Double Agent/seminar agent project, and the interaction between
the two agents he mentioned made me curious as well.

Overall, I am really interested in your work and wanted to kindly ask
whether you would have time to discuss potential opportunities to
contribute to your research?

Best regards,
Kaan Erdem

Yes this helps! I'll be able to riff on this. Thank you, Lydia :-)

On Wed, Aug 27, 2025 at 10:28 PM Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu>
wrote:

> well, I'm still brainstorming. In my mind, I use product management as the
> quintessential network-based approach. As an advisor of students, I see
> myself as a manager - I do all the finance, hiring, paperwork (IRBs),
> grants, visioning, and keeping currently projects on track. I feel like
> everyone understands management in that regard. JumpStarter is sort of a
> self-management tool.
>
> Typically, when you have a company and a goal - like launch a product -
> that requires coordination across multiple people to achieve it, that
> creates a network of processes.
>
> And you want to optimize for something like lowering the cost, launching
> before the CHI deadline, completely 12 user studies, etc :)
>
> Even administering a large class is a managerial problems.
>
> My go-to example for product management is to "Launch an customer service
> chatbot" for X. Often, I think about what if I launched a bot for my class
> - that answers students dumb questions like, where is the course website
> (PLEASE GOOGLE IT!!!)
>
> Does that help at all? I was hoping to have a stroke of genius, and full
> clarify, but shockingly that didn't happen :/
>
> On Wed, Aug 27, 2025 at 4:46 PM Andrew Head <head@seas.upenn.edu> wrote:
>
>> Hi Lydia! I'm spending a bit of time now and will also do so tomorrow
>> thinking through a potential technical network-based approach for the TRI
>> project. I wanted to ask if you'd move any closer to what you thought was a
>> good concrete

threads.subject: Re: I need to remake JumpStarter…

messages.subject: I need to remake JumpStarter…
messages.body:
Hi Lydia,

For sure! Please let me know if you have any questions:

1. First, I write a specification document that describes how the system
works, and I include original prompts or code that were mentioned in the
paper. (Below these steps, I've provided a prompt that you can use to get
AI to extract a simple spec from a research paper)

2. Then, I also give it screenshots from the paper that illustrate the
original UI and the overall architecture. For Jumpstarter, maybe I would
give it this:
[image: image.png][image: image.png]
[image: image.png]

3. Finally, I ask Cline to build me an end-to-end functional prototype of
the system described in my spec (in planning mode first) using this prompt: *I
want to rebuild an end-to-end working version of the JUMPSTARTER system.
Below, I outlined a simple spec that details the original system
implementation.*

If it has any follow-up questions for me, I give it the original paper in
pdf format and ask it to answer any questions it has by referencing the
paper, but to make sure it's still following my spec. Then, I toggle it to
agent mode and let it draft the first functional prototype. If needed, I
iterate slightly on certain components, and I help it debug by giving it
feedback from what I see in the console.

** Here's the most consistent prompt I've developed for using AI to extract
a simple spec from a research paper (I notice I still have to edit parts of
it sometimes):

*You need to extract a simple specification of the HCI system described in
the paper, which an LLM Agent like Cline could use to reconstruct an
end-to-end functional prototype.*

*The spec consists of 3 parts: written, visual, and code.  For more complex
systems, the "code" part of the spec can be embedded in the "written" part.
BUT THIS MUST BE WORD-FOR-WORD.*

*The written part of the spec should consist of clear, FULL SENTENCES that
step-by-step describe the workflow of the system.ALWAYS WRITE IN CLEAR,
FULL SENTENCES. USE WORDS LIKE 'FIRST', 'NEXT', 'THEN', 'FINALLY',ETC. TO
INDICATE THE ORDER OF THE STEPS.DO NOT NUMBER THE STEPS. DESCRIBE THE STEPS
IN FULL SENTENCES LIKE YOU WERE TALKING TO ANOTHER HUMAN.The visual part of
the spec should consist of at least one diagram that shows the system's
architecture. Ideally, this must be a screenshot of the system design or
original UI from the uploaded paper.If you cannot take a screenshot, then
you must prompt the user to provide a screenshot.*

*The "code" part of the spec should consist of all the ENTIRE EXACT
WORD-FOR-WORD original code snippets AND/OR prompts used to build the
original system that were provided in the paper. These are typically found
in the appendix.*

***CRITICAL INSTRUCTION:** I have already extracted the exact prompts and
code from this paper for you. They are provided below. You MUST use these
exact prompts word-for-word in your specification. DO NOT write "uses
prompt A.6" or "system uses prompt A.7" - instead, write out the FULL TEXT
of each prompt.*

*EXTRACTED PROMPTS AND CODE TO USE (EMBED THESE WORD-FOR-WORD):*

*''' + extracted_prompts + '''*

***ABSOLUTELY CRITICAL:** You are FORBIDDEN from using ANY of these
phrases:*

*- "[INSERT FULL TEXT OF PROMPT A.1]"*

*- "[INSERT FULL TEXT OF PROMPT A.3]" *

*- "[INSERT FULL TEXT OF PROMPTS A.4 AND A.5]"*

*- "[INSERT FULL TEXT OF PROMPTS A.6, A.7, A.8, A.9, A.10]"*

*- "as provided above"*

*- "as extracted above"*

*- "I've embedded the full text"*

*- "abbreviated them here for readability"*

***REQUIRED:** You MUST copy the ACTUAL TEXT from the extracted prompts
section above. Do NOT use placeholders. Do NOT abbreviate. Write out every
single word of each prompt.*

***EXAMPLE OF CORRECT FORMAT:***

*"Then the system uses this exact prompt: 'I'm learning how to
{content_type}. Analyze the following examples to identify clusters based
on STRUCTURAL and RHETORICAL patterns. Focus on how the content is
constructed and presented—not just what it's about. For textual examples,
examine rhetorica

messages.body:
3

On Sat, Jul 26, 2025, 6:56 PM Xuanming Zhang <billyzhang@cs.columbia.edu>
wrote:

> Got it, thanks! We just found this on the AAAI submission website
> <https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__aaai.org_conference_aaai_aaai-2D26_submission-2Dinstructions_&d=DwMFaQ&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=eTzyE3TuL3LOlGQcUzdJGPEzN4Q_xmPiPFqtpVUMkNo&m=whhgwCzPQtUQcjT7koPp2l-LPryrzIHfE7hY3ZXALyU27riXJL1JC_i0s9lcJlk6&s=dgE_ju9J050GoIxBi4EJ9CYLO_upZ-wDSfysM9QUcWA&e=>
> :
> [image: image.png]
> It seems like AAAI does not allow multiple submissions. In this case, what
> next step would you recommend?
>
> On Sat, Jul 26, 2025 at 2:43 PM Zhou Yu <zy2461@columbia.edu> wrote:
>
>> Option B.
>>
>> On Sat, Jul 26, 2025, 4:46 PM Xuanming Zhang <billyzhang@cs.columbia.edu>
>> wrote:
>>
>>> Hi Zhou,
>>>
>>> We just got the meta review from the ARR May cycle (see screenshot
>>> below). We got 2.5, and we are wondering if you could suggest possible next
>>> steps for JumpStarter.
>>>
>>> [image: image.png]
>>> Here are three options we could think up right now:
>>>
>>>    1. *Option A*: Commit to EMNLP (by Aug 1st); wait for
>>>    acceptance/rejection (by Aug 20th); If we get rejected, we submit it
>>>    to the HCI/NLP workshop
>>>    <https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__sites.google.com_view_hciandnlp&d=DwMFaQ&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=eTzyE3TuL3LOlGQcUzdJGPEzN4Q_xmPiPFqtpVUMkNo&m=-rYi39bHYBBcyEgug8HrGhUGDfqAcSKOMe-nCs_u359PACkNaKGb_olr40_jaEXa&s=zBwhvvnAzSw8sNWm6N8kulPDlHCBmM2nbG6_tqY8Zy4&e=>
>>>    co-located with EMNLP 2025 (decision made by Sep 8th - we can go
>>>    fast-track submissions with existing reviews)
>>>    2. *Option B: *Commit to EMNLP; also submit to AAAI 2026 (we have
>>>    submitted the abstract; full paper due Aug 1st). But the
>>>    key question is: is it allowed to submit a paper to AAAI while it is still
>>>    under the decision of acceptance of EMNLP? EMNLP mentions this: *“ARR
>>>    does not place any restrictions on committing to multiple venues
>>>    simultaneously, or committing to one venue and submitting for direct review
>>>    at another venue.”*
>>>    3. *Option C: *Forget about EMNLP, let’s submit it to AAAI 2026;
>>>    after the decision is made by Sep 8th, we can then decide if we want to
>>>    submit it to CHI or IUI for next year.
>>>
>>> Let us know your thoughts. Thanks!
>>>
>>> Best,
>>> Billy
>>>
>>

threads.subject: [ARR - May 2025] Reviewer JdBM commented on your submission. Paper Number: 4507, Paper Title: "JumpStarter: Human-AI Planning with Task-Structured Context Curation"

messages.subject: [ARR - May 2025] Reviewer JdBM commented on your submission. Paper Number: 4507, Paper Title: "JumpStarter: Human-AI Planning with Task-Structured Context Curation"
messages.body:
Reviewer JdBM commented on your submission.
    
Paper number: 4507

Paper title: JumpStarter: Human-AI Planning with Task-Structured Context Curation

Comment: Thanks for the discussion.

- referring to my comments, I understood that 6 participants (with each goal assigned to 2 participants) are expert evaluation, and the user study involves only 10 participants. The limited goals and participants limits the study's statistical robustness and generalizability. Also, the link provided in your rebuttal (reference#2 Why You Only Need to Test with 5 Users. Nielsen Norman Group) results in desk rejection, and this link is a blog rather than a peer reviewed academic work.
- ChatGPT or related LLMs offer interactive experience, and it is not fair to compare with ChatGPT without providing CVs, where most gains could come from extra content, e.g., CV in this work.

I intend to keep my score.

To view the comment, click here: https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__openreview.net_forum-3Fid-3Dg1LFZwFM5M-26noteId-3D0rYj6VmCeI&d=DwIFAg&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=hPTJFKhz3Hkwikm3mM2701VB1AZoIWoRMgOYWA3SHD0&m=zHD-MPwdRvoPp4JCHXpumDjCwlCgb4B6LpEqNQPfR6YP6f6hmrEefM8ReoZPcv4k&s=qEAyJwQbU_vRBSwBV2mnZ1iDGQZ488IKZrPqPelKtMs&e= 

Please note that responding to this email will direct your reply to support@aclrollingreview.org.

I think I briefly mentioned resubmitting our longitudinal workshop proposal

tell all future UIST people to consider using longitudinal as part of their
tools' eval.” H*ere’s the new workshop proposal
<https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__www.overleaf.com_4579944942rmmxhhvdhmnh-23edb643&d=DwIFaQ&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=-_LS5uo2o7-geJ5gnr0XLcB_sGrtzBYehOspkA3M8gk&m=LoK0j3eWPuy5Za55mIKb6bnnnpsnd1wRrNTcr8e5QYBOD3-5x7hmcs80Ic6Qwy3m&s=g1EtqGi7gvJLsaWF6gH18lpD5ZP5wvz6pnqlqTwi4i0&e= > i cut down to 2

>>> question together. He is refining the draft and will complete it within an
>>> hour and share it with you (no later than 6 p.m. EST).
>>>
>>> If possible, it'd be really helpful to have your eye on the draft
>>> (especially for the reviewer who gave us 2.5) before submission today.
>>> Thanks!!
>>>
>>> *Sitong Wang*
>>> Columbia University, PhD student in Computer Science
>>> sw3504@columbia.edu
>>>
>>>

threads.subject: [AAAI-26] Decision notification for your submission 22340: JUMPSTARTER: Human-AI Planning with Task-Structured Context Curation

messages.subject: [AAAI-26] Decision notification for your submission 22340: JUMPSTARTER: Human-AI Planning with Task-Structured Context Curation
messages.body:
Dear Lydia Chilton,

Thank you for your submission to the AAAI-26 Main Technical Track.  Your consideration offor AAAI-26 as a venue for publishing this work is greatly appreciated, as our community strives together to advance the frontiers of artificial intelligence.
 
We regret to inform you that this paper, JUMPSTARTER: Human-AI Planning with Task-Structured Context Curation (https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__openreview.net_forum-3Fid-3DOlpMvjrvBo&d=DwIFAg&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=hPTJFKhz3Hkwikm3mM2701VB1AZoIWoRMgOYWA3SHD0&m=HcDNzeXQrr00cUHqNBBgU2t7Z3c7zBb8EGv76r66V1_8apsh_Rrw-1UHDlng6Qdj&s=pAgg58UisP2RdkxOoPLoMb9w05DQ7-Uqmy9XSNLV7ZI&e= ), did not advance to Phase 2 review for the AAAI-26 conference.  The paper was reviewed by at least two human reviewers in Phase 1, and unfortunately it did not garner sufficient support for promotion to Phase 2. This decision is final. The provided reviews contain more detail, which we hope will be beneficial to the progress of this research.

We appreciate the effort involved in preparing this paper, and we understand the disappointment that comes from such a decision. The AAAI-26 review process provided reviews to nearly 23,000 submissions to the Main Technical Track.  While the growth seen by AAAI-26 is a welcomed sign, there were many difficult decisions made in this Phase 1 to ensure the review process provides timely reviews with quality feedback.

We welcome community input on the review process. Please either complete [this form](https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__aaaiforms.wufoo.com_forms_qvutnra0ffzhz6_&d=DwIFAg&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=hPTJFKhz3Hkwikm3mM2701VB1AZoIWoRMgOYWA3SHD0&m=HcDNzeXQrr00cUHqNBBgU2t7Z3c7zBb8EGv76r66V1_8apsh_Rrw-1UHDlng6Qdj&s=JcyxoV-91wZ3gmF7mQrHF-QBxbhoNwjyY5P4iiCzm-g&e= ) or bring your suggestions to the **AAAI-26 Community Meeting in Singapore**. We hope that you will be able to participate in AAAI-26 for January in Singapore. Our Program Team remains committed to our highest priority for assembling an outstanding technical program for AAAI-26.

We look forward to your continued progress in the advancement of AI and your contributions to future AAAI conferences. 

Sincerely,

Odest Chadwicke Jenkins, University of Michigan
Matthew E. Taylor, University of Alberta
AAAI-26 Program Chairs

Bo An, Nanyang Technological University
Joydeep Biswas, University of Texas at Austin
David J. Crandall, Indiana University
Matthew Lease, University of Texas at Austin
Kiri L. Wagstaff, Oregon State University
AAAI-26 Associate Program Chairs

Please note that responding to this email will direct your reply to workflowchairs26@aaai.zendesk.com.

threads.subject: AAAI-26 has received a new revision of your submission titled JUMPSTARTER: Human-AI Planning with Task-Structured Context Curation

messages.subject: AAAI-26 has received a new revision of your submission titled JUMPSTARTER: Human-AI Planning with Task-Structured Context Curation
messages.body:
Your new revision of the submission to AAAI-26 has been posted.

Title: JUMPSTARTER: Human-AI Planning with Task-Structured Context Curation

Abstract Human-AI planning for complex goals remains challenging with current large language models (LLMs), which rely on linear chat histories and simplistic memory mechanisms. Despite advances in long-context prompting, users still manually manage information, leading to a high cognitive burden. Hence, we propose JumpStarter, a system that enables LLMs to collaborate with humans on complex goals by dynamically decomposing tasks to help users manage context. We specifically introduce task-structured context curation, a novel framework that breaks down a user’s goal into a hierarchy of actionable subtasks, and scopes context to localized decision points, enabling finer-grained personalization and reuse. The framework is realized through three core mechanisms: context elicitation, selection, and reuse. We demonstrate that task-structured context curation significantly improves plan quality by 16% over ablations. Our user study shows that JumpStarter helped users generate plans with 79% higher quality compared to ChatGPT.

To view your submission, click here: https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__openreview.net_forum-3Fid-3DOlpMvjrvBo&d=DwIFaQ&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=hPTJFKhz3Hkwikm3mM2701VB1AZoIWoRMgOYWA3SHD0&m=YpDJCnBZqLORhfvsjCLDCn_tp3bLU7raxjIxdzqkNEhJoSzph6S9PDMc15nPmyyk&s=YwaHCnEFKdom6BEXkYUEFntCDdWTFQTx2LqAbPWJxtw&e= 

Please note that responding to this email will direct your reply to workflowchairs26@aaai.zendesk.com.

messages.body:
Your submission to AAAI-26 has been posted.

Submission Number: 22340 

Title: JUMPSTARTER: Human-AI Planning with Task-Structured Context Curation 

Abstract: Human-AI planning for complex goals remains challenging with current large language models (LLMs), which rely on linear chat histories and simplistic memory mechanisms. Despite advances in long-context prompting, users still manually manage information, leading to a high cognitive burden. Hence, we propose JumpStarter, a system that enables LLMs to collaborate with humans on complex goals by dynamically decomposing tasks to help users manage context. We specifically introduce task-structured context curation, a novel framework that breaks down a user’s goal into a hierarchy of actionable subtasks, and scopes context to localized decision points, enabling finer-grained personalization and reuse. The framework is realized through three core mechanisms: context elicitation, selection, and reuse. We demonstrate that task-structured context curation significantly improves plan quality by 16% over ablations. Our user study shows that JumpStarter helped users generate plans with 79% higher quality compared to ChatGPT. 

To view your submission, click here: https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__openreview.net_forum-3Fid-3DOlpMvjrvBo&d=DwIFaQ&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=hPTJFKhz3Hkwikm3mM2701VB1AZoIWoRMgOYWA3SHD0&m=uPOWOWzUUD3AhZVoQm_ZdAGGCSdKeUVPAgefSbletsnQdjBwSi2ijNzJ40Mo25ZA&s=3HfDXsv7q-ClSW6CUloiEHpvwgEIhEUM3oBIastbJFE&e=

messages.subject: [EXT] Re: Visiting NYC (May 31–June 7): Meeting Request on HCI Postdoc Opportunities
messages.body:
Dr. Chilton,

Thanks for your reply!

No worries and I hope your time in Europe goes well.

I agree! Emphasized by Large Language Objects<https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__interactions.acm.org_archive_view_july-2Daugust-2D2024_large-2Dlanguage-2Dobjects-2Dthe-2Ddesign-2Dof-2Dphysical-2Dai-2Dand-2Dgenerative-2Dexperiences&d=DwIGaQ&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=hPTJFKhz3Hkwikm3mM2701VB1AZoIWoRMgOYWA3SHD0&m=YpEjdAa_uFTTy783Wjqb7Q4NbyClu70LNTkDcfeOxF-e3cJgvmuWKmHGLsx_JQ7m&s=RcK2q-Y4xXS7zZuiQDildPOB8F0RdUFHV90_vA52pjQ&e= > coming into the mix, I’ve had it in my mind for a while to run a workshop related to designing for context, especially in at-home scenarios. It’s a great time to get new perspectives in that area.

Thanks again for your time and I hope we run into each other at a conference!

Thanks,
James Spann

From: Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu>
Date: Monday, June 2, 2025 at 7:35 AM
To: Spann, James <jspann2@cs.rochester.edu>
Subject: [EXT] Re: Visiting NYC (May 31–June 7): Meeting Request on HCI Postdoc Opportunities
Hi James- I'm actually in Europe all summer for bizarre and confounding personal reasons. I may be here much of next year as well, so I'm not taking any new members to my lab this coming year.

But studying context is set to be the next big thing - given the fast adoption of MCP. I think there could be a lot of people interested in this, even who haven't published or worked on it yet.

Best of luck!
lydia
