Good afternoon Dr. Chilton,

My name is James Spann, and I am a PhD candidate in Human–Computer Interaction at University of Rochester, advised by Dr. Zhen Bai. My current dissertation work is focused on two longitudinal usability studies for hearing parents and their DHH children. I have been following your work on context and content aware creative design tools and was excited to see your recent CHI workshop and projects like JumpStarter that try to take work in this area further.

I will be in the NYC area from May 31 through June 7 and would greatly appreciate the opportunity to meet with you, or a member of your lab, for 30 minutes to discuss potential postdoctoral opportunities and learn more about the future direction of your group’s work. My availability is:

  *   June 1, all day
  *   June 2, any time after 2p
  *   June 3, unavailable
  *   June 4, any time after 2p
  *   June 5, all day except 3-4p
  *   June 6, all day
  *   June 7, all day

I realize this is short notice, so if an in-person meeting is not feasible, I would be happy to arrange a brief Zoom call at your convenience!

Thanks,
James Spann
PhD Candidate @ University of Rochester

threads.subject: Progress Update and Summer Plans

messages.subject: Progress Update and Summer Plans
messages.body:
Hi Zhou and Lydia,

I hope you're both doing well! I wanted to share a brief update on my
recent work and plans for the summer.

Over the past semester, I’ve been primarily focused on the DoubleAgent
project. I’ve attached a report I wrote for Eugene’s agent class, which
outlines our current progress in detail. Please feel free to take a look.
Here’s a summary of my contributions so far:

   - System backend development: implemented simulation agents (test case
   generation) and coordination agents (planning and executing actions)
   - System interface design: prototyped the interface and co-developed the
   front-end UI with Tao
   - User studies: conducted pilot user testing and formative studies
   - System evaluation: performed functional testing and technical
   evaluations

For the summer, I plan to continue collaborating with Tao to wrap up the
DoubleAgent project. Our goal is to prepare a submission and upload it to
arXiv by mid-June. In parallel, I’ll be working full-time on my internship
project with Dan Roth at Oracle. I’ll make sure to keep you updated on that
project as well.

Please let me know if you have any questions or feedback!

Best,
Billy

messages.body:
Yes, we can do TA and third year support. Thanks Lydia for all the context.

On Mon, May 26, 2025, 7:08 AM Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu> wrote:

> I'm split on Xuanming right now. He's doing a lot better than he was. He
> definitely put in the work this semester - which I was happy about. I'm ok
> with his progress at the moment.
>
> I do find that generally, he's not very good at articulating what he
> thinks. But I'm surprised that he had trouble articulating
> the contribution, because I do think he knows what the contribution of the
> system is.
>
> It's possible that he had trouble explaining the contribution because he
> left for his internship when we were "rethinking" the contribution. He and
> Tao ran an evaluation that wasn't quite right for the system. (I blame Tao
> more for that). The evaluation actually made the system look better than it
> was, and showed them why, and so now we have to go back and redo the
> evaluation carefully. I actually think the contribution will stay the same,
> in the end, we just have to take it slower. They were certainly rushing the
> project to meet the UIST deadline. I do feel like there's some critical
> thinking that's missing.
>
> He was pretty terrible at the beginning of the double agents project. He
> really struggled to make abstract concepts concrete, even simple ones. And
> because we had such a slow start, he had to leave before the project was
> over.
>
> I think he did a good job of re-framing the JumpStarter project from an
> NLP perspective, so that was valuable to me, personally.
>
> For me, because he has improved so much lately, I'm overall happy with
> him. Even if he's not the best HCI or NLP student, he's still valuable to
> me as a bridge between the fields. And he's been a good participant in the
> DAPLab group lunches.
>
> Next year, maybe he can be funded by the TA support and the 3rd year
> residency program, we can see if he uses that time to improve?
>
>

threads.subject: ARR - May 2025 has received your submission titled JumpStarter: Human-AI Planning with Task-Structured Context Curation

messages.subject: ARR - May 2025 has received your submission titled JumpStarter: Human-AI Planning with Task-Structured Context Curation
messages.body:
Your submission to ARR - May 2025 has been updated.

Submission Number: 4507 

Title: JumpStarter: Human-AI Planning with Task-Structured Context Curation 

Abstract: Human-AI planning for complex goals remains challenging with current large language models (LLMs), which rely on linear chat histories and simplistic memory mechanisms. Despite advances in long-context prompting, users still manually manage information, leading to a high cognitive burden. Hence, we propose JumpStarter, a system that enables LLMs to collaborate with humans on complex goals by dynamically decomposing tasks to help users manage context. We specifically introduce task-structured context curation, a novel framework that breaks down a user’s goal into a hierarchy of actionable subtasks, and scopes context to localized decision points, enabling finer-grained personalization and reuse. The framework is realized through three core mechanisms: context elicitation, selection, and reuse. We demonstrate that task-structured context curation significantly improves plan quality by 16\% over ablations. Our user study shows that JumpStarter helped users generate plans with 79% higher quality compared to ChatGPT. 

To view your submission, click here: https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__openreview.net_forum-3Fid-3Dg1LFZwFM5M&d=DwIFaQ&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=hPTJFKhz3Hkwikm3mM2701VB1AZoIWoRMgOYWA3SHD0&m=E_3Xfr25jkE9M-C8CGvwtZ6fB5vieOAxr6aQlHcrFuCahrmM--i-AhiITnVoV3Cs&s=kuG5yKIzokYcMgv6LW9I8DDoGXL9R201tNMDXMq82OQ&e= 

If you are not an author of this submission and would like to be removed, please contact the author who added you at billyzhang24kobe@gmail.com

Please note that responding to this email will direct your reply to support@aclrollingreview.org.

threads.subject: Re: where are we on jumpstarter?

messages.subject: where are we on jumpstarter?
messages.body:
Hi Lydia,

Billy and I waited for 15 minutes for the 10:00 a.m. meeting, but didn’t
see you, so we ended the call (since it was already 1:00 a.m. in Seattle
for Billy). And no worries at all - it's on me, as I sent the invite very
late.

Since Billy is the main person working on the current version of the
abstract and introduction, I think it's important to have him in the
meeting to discuss the abstract/intro framing.

During our brief 15-minute chat, Billy mentioned that Zhou found the
previous version of the abstract clear. However, we two agreed that the
task decomposition aspect should be emphasized more. We also thought it
might be helpful to add a sentence explaining that the subtask tree
representation is a prerequisite for task-structured context curation (the
expression can be better, but hopefully the idea is right).

Billy will be available to meet anytime starting at 12:00 p.m. Seattle time
(9:00 p.m. Skopje time) since it’s his internship orientation day. I’ve
sent a new invite - please let us know if that works for you.

In the meantime, I’ll continue proofreading the draft and improving the
language and expression. Please let us know if the revised teaser figure
<https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1Avl5K5b2ZpMdK7A4QLSFVTlgPnC4aj9G/edit?usp=sharing&ouid=111688875234277592389&rtpof=true&sd=true >
makes sense. Also, if you think a meeting between just the two of us would
be helpful, feel free to let me know - I'm all available.

Best,
Sitong

On Sun, May 18, 2025 at 7:51 PM Xuanming Zhang <billyzhang@cs.columbia.edu>
wrote:

> I just made a small edit to on slide 2. I feel the labels are much clearer
> than before!
>
> I can meet at 10 AM Skopje time as Sitong suggested :) The deadline is May
> 19th AOE at 11:59pm, and I will try to make one submission tomorrow after
> we finalize some changes (e.g. the figures). We have cut the pages to fit
> the 8-page limit as well!
>
> Lydia - For the new abstract, I think calling JumpStarter a framework is a
> bit inconsistent with what we discussed in the intro and system sections.
> Maybe task-structured context curation is a framework we propose; and
> JumpStarter is the system that we build to implement the framework for
> people to use? Let me know if that makes sense to you, and sorry if I miss
> something.
>
> Billy
>
> On Sun, May 18, 2025 at 1:30 PM Sitong Wang <sw3504@columbia.edu> wrote:
>
>> Sorry I somehow just saw your responses.
>>
>> I totally agree. I tried a new version of Figure 1 - mainly updated the
>> labels -  I’m feeling a bit stuck with the current visual layout. @Billy
>> please feel free to copy paste and edit over it:
>>
>>
>> https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1Avl5K5b2ZpMdK7A4QLSFVTlgPnC4aj9G/edit?usp=sharing&ouid=111688875234277592389&rtpof=true&sd=true 
>> I’m flexible to meet anytime that works for you! I'm sharing the same
>> Airbnb with my parents, but I assume they'll be super jet-lagged. How about
>> a quick meeting at 10 AM Skopje time (4 AM NYC / 1 AM Seattle)? Not sure if
>> that might be too much for Billy though as I guess he's still in NYC time
>> zone :-)
>>
>> *Sitong Wang*
>> Columbia University, PhD student in Computer Science
>> sw3504@columbia.edu
>>
>>
>> On Sun, May 18, 2025 at 3:45 PM Xuanming Zhang <
>> billyzhang@cs.columbia.edu> wrote:
>>
>>> Sounds good. I just finished editing all sections including the
>>> appendix. Will also take a look at figure 1 later today. I can meet perhaps
>>> after 6pm PST today. I can also do other time if you prefer.
>>>
>>> Billy
>>>
>>> On Sun, May 18, 2025 at 12:31 Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu>
>>> wrote:
>>>
>>>> When can you meet? I'm free from 10am Skopje time.
>>>>
>>>> On Sun, May 18, 2025 at 21:30 Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu>
>>>> wrote:
>>>>
>>>>> Yes! Alsobring out the heirarchical representation of the task tree.
>>>>> Maybe read my new abstract? The words "task structured context curation"
>>>>> are cool, but it doesn't quiet fully explain itself. We

Columbia Engineering Lecture Series in AI

November 21: Jingren Zhou, CTO Alibaba Cloud

   - 10:30-11:00am Check-in
   - 11:00-12:00pm Lecture

*Advance registration is required for both Columbia affiliates and
non-affiliates*

*ABOUT THE LECTURE*

"Building Foundation Models at Scale: System Experiences and Challenges"

The rapid evolution of AI has led to the emergence of massive and complex
foundation models that require enormous computational resources, making
efficient training and inference systems essential. Training such models
requires large-scale distributed computation, effective overlap of
computation and communication, sophisticated parallelization strategies,
and robust fault-tolerant mechanisms. Inference systems, on the other hand,
must support diverse workloads with varying service-level agreements
(SLAs), rapidly integrate engineering optimizations, and carefully balance
trade-offs among throughput, latency, cost, and availability, particularly
in distributed environments. In this talk, I will discuss the major systems
challenges in building large-scale foundation models, focusing on our
experiences developing Qwen (large language models) and Wan (video
generative models). I will also present ongoing research and system designs
that enhance the efficiency of training and inference at scale, enabling
more effective management of complex AI workloads in cloud environments.

*ABOUT THE SPEAKER*

Jingren Zhou is the Chief Technology Officer at Alibaba Cloud, where he
drives technology innovation and product development across a wide range of
cloud computing services. He also leads the development of AI foundation
models, such as Qwen and Wan models, and their applications in diverse
business applications within Alibaba Cloud. Prior to this role, he played a
key role in building Alibaba’s cloud-scale distributed data analytics
platform and developing advanced techniques for personalized search,
product recommendation, and advertising on Alibaba’s e-commerce platform.
Before joining Alibaba, he was a veteran at Microsoft, focusing on big data
and database research and development. His research interests include cloud
computing, distributed systems, databases, and large-scale machine
learning. He has served as PC co-chair and core committee member for many
academic conferences and technical forums. He received his PhD in Computer
Science from Columbia University. He is a Fellow of ACM and IEEE.
Register Here

threads.subject: Prospective PhD Student – Interested in Multi-Agent Reasoning and Human-Centered AI Systems

messages.subject: Prospective PhD Student – Interested in Multi-Agent Reasoning and Human-Centered AI Systems
messages.body:
Dear Professor Chilton,

My name is Jiaxun Zhang, and I am currently a senior majoring in Computer Science and Statistics at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign. I am writing to express my strong interest in applying to your lab for a PhD position in Fall 2026, focusing on focusing on multi-agent reasoning and human centered AI systems.

I have been especially inspired by your work on “AgentDynEx: Nudging the Mechanics and Dynamics of Multi-Agent Simulations” and “Simulating Strategic Reasoning: Comparing the Ability of Single LLMs and Multi-Agent Systems to Replicate Human Behavior”. I am fascinated by how your research formalizes the balance between structure and emergence—developing frameworks that combine mechanics and dynamics to produce interpretable, human-aligned behaviors. Your systematic approach to steering creativity and reasoning in agentic systems deeply resonates with my own goal of developing reflective and reliable AI frameworks that bridge reasoning, safety, and design.

At UIUC, I work with Prof. Jiaxuan You on SafeScientist (EMNLP 2025, Recommend Oral), a framework for risk-aware scientific discovery by multi-agent LLMs, integrating layered safety modules and semantic-rule auditing to improve ethical compliance by 44%. I also co-led MedVL-R1 (under review), developing a tool-augmented medical vision-language reasoner using reinforcement learning to enhance multimodal interpretability, and contributed to Replika Mental Health (under review), which examines emotional adaptation and behavioral alignment of conversational LLMs in long-term human–AI interactions. Currently, through the NCSA SPIN Fellowship, I am leading SciFy, which builds a multi-turn DPO alignment pipeline for scientific claim verification, exploring how multi-agent reasoning and iterative feedback can enhance factual reliability. In parallel, I am developing OUTSegHallu: Counterfactual Visual Reasoning for Segmentation Hallucination Evaluation and Mitigation, a framework that systematically analyzes and mitigates hallucination in vision–language segmentation models through counterfactual reasoning and structured intervention.

I would be very grateful if you could let me know whether you are considering new PhD students for Fall 2026. I have attached my CV for your reference. Thank you very much for your time and consideration, and I would be honored to discuss potential research opportunities in your group.

Best,
Jiaxun (Olivia) Zhang
B.S. in Computer Science & Statistics
University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign
